findings.title: Track all creator KPIs (Instagram, OF metrics) in Notion to identify what's working
findings.claim: Having a Notion database that tracks everything — Instagram performance, OF metrics — lets you quickly identify what's working and what isn't across creators.
findings.what_they_do: Jeppe tracks all creator activity in Notion (Instagram stats, OF performance, content outputs). This single source of truth replaces ad-hoc mental tracking and lets the operator and team know at a glance where each creator stands.
findings.vs_us: Palm tracks content and assets in Airtable (§1, §9) and has whale-hunting/fan-tracker CRM (§9), but no documented unified creator performance dashboard that combines social + OF metrics. Notion is not part of Palm's current stack.
findings.recommendation: Build or extend a unified creator performance view in Airtable (Palm's existing spine) that surfaces IG growth, OF revenue, and content output per creator in one place — mirrors the value Jeppe gets from Notion without adding a new tool.

sources.quote: on notion like track everything like you know, Instagram off like everything is tracked so you know what you're doing right and what you're doing wrong

findings.topic: Content testing framework for new creators
findings.title: Run 4–5 content style tests per creator over 3–4 weeks before doubling down
findings.claim: When onboarding a new creator, test 4–5 content types simultaneously for 3–4 weeks, then identify and double down only on what is gaining traction.
findings.what_they_do: After identifying a creator's niche, Jeppe's team studies top competitors in that niche, then runs 4–5 different content style variants for roughly three weeks to one month. They track performance and cut losers, scaling only the winning style.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content engine (§1) is inspo-driven and data-backed, but the pipeline is focused on pulling viral reels from inspo accounts and adapting them — there is no documented structured testing sprint for new creator onboarding that explicitly runs N styles in parallel for a defined window before committing to a direction.
findings.recommendation: Formalize a new-creator content sprint: after onboarding, run 4–5 style variants (drawn from Palm's inspo pipeline) for 3–4 weeks, score by engagement, then lock the winning style as the creator's primary content direction. Add a 'Content Sprint' status to the Airtable Inspiration or Assets table.

sources.quote: we're going to look to the big competitors and put the model in this system of testing content like four or five types of content for like three one three weeks, one month and see what's working and just push what's working

findings.topic: Creator plug-in model vs. agency plug-in
findings.title: Build a fixed agency system that creators plug into — not a custom setup per creator
findings.claim: Agencies that scale do so because creators are plugged into the agency's pre-built system rather than the agency rebuilding its process around each new creator.
findings.what_they_do: Jeppe and Griffin explicitly frame the agency as a productized system (content testing process, chatting setup, marketing team structure) that any new creator is onboarded into wholesale. The system does not change per creator — the creator adapts to it.
findings.vs_us: Palm has strong productized elements (§1 content pipeline, §4 warmup cadence for AI accounts), but real-creator onboarding (§6) is thin and largely Josh-DM-led with no documented plug-in process. There is no written 'here is what every creator enters when they sign with Palm.'
findings.recommendation: Define and document Palm's real-creator 'plug-in kit': the fixed sequence every signed creator goes through (IG audit → content sprint → chatting handoff → KPI baseline). This becomes the sales asset in acquisition and the ops SOP in onboarding — directly addresses §6 GAP.

sources.quote: for the creators whenever you sign models you got a system in place like a content that you're going to make this model run through like this process you know it's way easy

findings.title: Run 3–4 TikTok and Instagram accounts per creator to multiply distribution surface
findings.claim: Publishing the same creator's content across 3–4 accounts on both TikTok and Instagram simultaneously dramatically increases top-of-funnel reach compared to a single account.
findings.what_they_do: Jeppe's VA team publishes content every day across 3–4 TikTok accounts and 3–4 Instagram accounts per creator. Content research is handled by two dedicated people; VAs handle the actual publishing across all accounts daily.
findings.vs_us: Palm's current posting infrastructure (§5) routes through Publer and Amin (manual posting contractor) but is not documented as running multiple accounts per creator at scale. The AI-persona program (§3/§4) does build multiple isolated accounts, but for real creators this multi-account distribution strategy is a GAP (§5).
findings.recommendation: For real creators with growth headroom, test spinning up 2–3 additional IG/TikTok accounts per creator and routing daily content to all of them via Publer. Document volume limits and warmup requirements to avoid platform flags — leverage Palm's existing AI-account warmup playbook (§4) as a template.

sources.quote: VAs that are going to publish the content across just two platform. We've just been doing TikTok and Instagram like with a lot of account, not just one. It's like three, four accounts. And they publish content every day.

findings.topic: Chatting agency as first major hire
findings.title: Outsource chatting to an agency immediately — it's the single biggest revenue unlock
findings.claim: Plugging in a third-party chatting agency (rather than managing chatters in-house) was the most impactful hire for scaling from $5K to $30K/month.
findings.what_they_do: Jeppe uses a dedicated external chatting agency for both creators. He does not run his own chatters. This outsourcing freed him to focus on marketing and growth rather than DM management, and he credits the chatting team alongside his marketing team as the two things that changed his business.
findings.vs_us: Palm already outsources chatting to an external team (§8) — this mirrors Jeppe's model exactly. However, Palm's roadmap includes bringing chatting in-house (~1yr) and building AI chatting. This finding validates the current outsourced approach as industry-standard and confirms the in-house build-toward is the right next step, not an urgent pivot.
findings.recommendation: No change needed for current state — Palm's outsourced model is confirmed best practice at this revenue tier. For the in-house roadmap (§8 build-toward), note that Jeppe's agency did NOT transition in-house at $30K/month — Palm should set a higher revenue threshold (e.g. $100K+/month or 5+ creators) before the transition makes economic sense.

sources.quote: I think my chatting agency and definitely my marketing team. I mean these two things like change the business.

findings.topic: Content research role as first in-house hire
findings.title: Hire two dedicated content researchers before scaling VAs — separating research from publishing
findings.claim: Splitting content research (finding what to make) from content publishing (posting it) into two distinct roles is what enables consistent daily multi-platform output without operator involvement.
findings.what_they_do: Jeppe's marketing team structure: two people doing content research for the creator (studying competitors, identifying styles to test), plus separate VAs who only publish the content across platforms daily. The operator does not touch either function.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) already separates inspo scraping (automated via Apify) from publishing (Publer/Amin), which is structurally similar. However, the human 'content research' role — someone actively studying competitor accounts and selecting what to promote — is done by leadership (Josh/Evan) rather than a dedicated hire. This is a scaling bottleneck.
findings.recommendation: Define a 'Content Researcher' role spec: responsible for reviewing Palm's inspo queue daily, promoting the strongest reels to creator boards, and identifying niche-specific competitor accounts to add as inspo sources. This is the first hire that frees leadership from the §1 pipeline's review step.

sources.quote: it's basically two guys that do content research for the model. And of course, VAs that are going to publish the content across just two platform.

findings.title: At $30K/month with two creators, revenue concentration in one creator ($20K) is a key fragility
findings.claim: Running only two creators where one generates 67% of total revenue ($20K of $30K) creates dangerous concentration risk that limits agency valuation and resilience.
findings.what_they_do: Jeppe's $30K/month comes from two creators: one at $20K and one at $10K. This is presented positively in the video, but the structure means losing the top creator cuts revenue by two-thirds. No mention of a third creator pipeline or diversification plan.
findings.vs_us: No internal baseline documented on revenue concentration thresholds or creator diversification targets (§7, §0 GAP). Palm's current model also runs a small number of real creators. This is a shared risk.
findings.recommendation: Set an internal policy: no single creator should represent more than 40% of agency gross revenue at steady state. When a creator crosses that threshold, trigger active acquisition outreach (§6) to sign the next creator. Document this as a KPI in the Palm ops dashboard.

sources.quote: one is making 20k per month and the other one around 10

findings.title: Set realistic earnings expectations at signing to prevent churn
findings.claim: Models leave because they have unrealistic earnings expectations (e.g. celebrity-level income) that agencies fail to correct at onboarding.
findings.what_they_do: Kyle advises agencies to explicitly address and reset model expectations during signing — acknowledging that earnings benchmarks they've seen (celebrities making millions) are not representative, and that results scale with agency experience over time.
findings.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard capturing commission %, contract, and voice memo (§6) but has no documented step for earnings expectation-setting or realistic benchmarking conversation. This is a GAP (§6, §7).
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit 'earnings expectations' touchpoint to the Palm onboarding flow — a short script or written disclosure that frames realistic month-1 projections, references what factors drive earnings, and avoids implied guarantees. Could be embedded in the voice memo step or as a dedicated onboarding call agenda item.

sources.quote: a lot of models have unrealistic expectations so they follow a lot of celebrities that have started running fans

findings.title: Never guarantee a specific earnings figure you haven't proven you can deliver
findings.claim: Agencies that promise specific income figures (e.g. '$10K/month guaranteed') and fail to deliver are seen as scammers, causing immediate model churn.
findings.what_they_do: Kyle's rule: never state a dollar guarantee unless you have already achieved it for another model. Instead, use under-promise / over-deliver framing — commit to effort and process, not outcome figures.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented outbound pitch script or acquisition SOP (§6 GAP). Without a written acquisition script, individual reps may be making ad-hoc income guarantees. Palm's commission model is also undocumented (§7 GAP), meaning there's no internal anchor to even know what's realistic to promise.
findings.recommendation: Write a Palm acquisition script that explicitly excludes specific monthly earnings guarantees. Replace with process-based commitments ('we will run X, Y, Z for you') and reference ranges only where Palm has verified data. Pair with an internal earnings benchmark tracker so reps know what's actually been achieved.

sources.quote: don't make that guarantee if you cannot fulfill that guarantee if you have never scaled a girl to 10K per month

findings.topic: model retention — competition from larger agencies
findings.title: Compete on relationship quality and team culture, not budget or brand name
findings.claim: Better-funded agencies will always exist, so the retention lever smaller agencies have is making models feel they belong to a genuine team that no large agency can replicate.
findings.what_they_do: Kyle recommends becoming genuine friends with models, making them feel maximally comfortable, and cultivating a 'team feel' that big-budget agencies sacrifice at scale. The goal is making the model believe there is no better alternative for how she is treated.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator success cadence or relationship management SOP (§9 GAP — no written retention strategy). Palm's current model is transactional (invoice + commission). There is no structured check-in, milestone celebration, or creator NPS/satisfaction process.
findings.recommendation: Define a Palm creator success cadence: e.g. weekly Slack/Telegram check-in, monthly performance review call, milestone acknowledgements (first $1K, first $5K months). Small gestures that signal Palm is a team, not a vendor. Assign a named 'creator success' owner from existing staff.

sources.quote: treat your models the best as you can become genuine friends or friends with your models make them feel as comfortable as possible

findings.topic: model retention — creator autonomy and comfort
findings.title: Never impose mandatory content types or rigid working hours on creators
findings.claim: Creators leave agencies that dictate what content to make or when to work, because freedom is the primary reason they chose OnlyFans.
findings.what_they_do: Kyle frames the agency role as a service provider, not an employer. The agency provides marketing, systems, and monetization; the creator controls content type, schedule, and comfort boundaries. Agencies that 'boss girls about' lose them.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) drives content *ideation* from viral data and promotes filming ideas to creators — this is already framed as suggestions, not mandates. However, Palm has no documented creator-facing SOP clarifying what is required vs. suggested, which could create friction if a creator perceives the inspo-driven pipeline as prescriptive.
findings.recommendation: Add a one-page 'how we work together' document for creators that clearly frames the inspo pipeline as suggestions ('here are ideas that are working — film what feels right to you'), not directives. Clarify what Palm actually requires (e.g. minimum post frequency) vs. what is optional. Reduces misalignment before it becomes a retention issue.

sources.quote: Don't Force girls to create content they don't want to do, don't force girls to work hours they don't want to work

findings.title: Match acquisition targets to expected earnings tolerance by country tier
findings.claim: First-world-country models have higher earnings expectations and lower patience for underperformance, while models from lower-income countries are more tolerant of slower ramp-ups.
findings.what_they_do: Kyle segments model acquisition by geography: US/UK/AU models expect large, fast results and will churn quickly if peers appear to earn more; models from lower-income countries have lower baseline expectations and give agencies more runway to prove value.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented targeting criteria (§6 GAP). There is no written model qualification framework by geography, earnings expectation tier, or experience level. Palm likely signs US/UK creators by default without a structured expectation conversation calibrated to that tier.
findings.recommendation: Add a geography/expectation tier field to the creator onboarding qualification checklist. For US/UK/AU creators, build a specific expectation-calibration script that addresses the peer-comparison risk (other creators they know making more) and reframes the Palm value prop around systems and consistency rather than speed-to-high-income.

sources.quote: if you're hiring from third world countries or just poorer countries in general they're going to be more understanding and they're going to think oh they're actually doing a job

findings.title: Establish explicit professional conduct standards to prevent creator discomfort
findings.claim: Agencies where staff behave unprofessionally or make creators uncomfortable lose those creators, and this is a systemic failure that needs to be addressed at the hiring and onboarding level.
findings.what_they_do: Kyle frames the agency as a legitimate professional services company. He argues any staff member who treats OF management as an opportunity for personal gratification or control over women will cause creator churn. The fix is cultural and structural: hire people who see it as a business, not a lifestyle perk.
findings.vs_us: Palm's team is small (§10: Josh, Evan, Amin, editors, external chat team) with no documented code of conduct, hiring criteria, or staff-to-creator interaction guidelines. As Palm scales and adds editors, chatters, and other roles with creator access, this becomes an increasing risk. No internal baseline exists (§10 GAP).
findings.recommendation: Write a Palm staff conduct policy covering: (1) how staff communicate with creators, (2) what topics/requests are out of scope, (3) escalation path if a creator reports discomfort. Embed this into the hiring/onboarding checklist for any role that has direct creator contact (editors, future in-house chatters). Low effort, high retention insurance.

sources.quote: this business model was about money it's about actual business and professionalism

findings.title: SFW Reddit accounts unlock 'Suggested For You' organic reach vs NSFW-only posting
findings.claim: Posting from a Safe-For-Work Reddit account allows content to appear in Reddit's 'Suggested For You' feed, reaching users outside your subscribed subreddits — something NSFW accounts cannot do.
findings.what_they_do: They use SFW-flagged Reddit accounts (bought or converted by deleting NSFW posts) to post creator content into SFW niche subreddits, enabling algorithmic distribution to non-followers via Reddit's suggested feed. NSFW accounts are confined only to their subreddit's existing members.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit traffic strategy (§5 🔲 GAP — traffic/distribution not documented). This is a concrete, zero-ad-spend organic traffic channel Palm is not running.
findings.recommendation: Test the SFW Reddit method for at least 2–3 real creators. Acquire or convert aged (30+ day, 200+ karma) SFW accounts, pick a niche (gym, sports) that fits each creator, and track upvote growth week-over-week in a Google Sheet. Prioritize creators whose content lends itself to niche SFW communities (fitness, lifestyle).

sources.quote: with the safe for work method, you can be basically seen by anyone, even though they might not be in the same group or in the same subreddit

findings.title: Reddit SFW accounts must be 30+ days old with 200+ karma before posting creator content
findings.claim: A SFW Reddit account needs to be at least 30 days old with a few hundred karma minimum to post effectively without triggering bans or suppression.
findings.what_they_do: They either purchase aged SFW accounts from Reddit account sellers or convert existing NSFW accounts by deleting all NSFW posts. The account must meet the age/karma floor before any creator content is posted.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no Reddit account acquisition or management process documented (§5 🔲 GAP). Palm does have a hardware-isolated warmup system for AI social accounts (§4 MATURE for AI) — a similar structured warmup SOP could be adapted for Reddit.
findings.recommendation: Source aged SFW Reddit accounts (30+ days, 200+ karma) from account vendors. Build a Reddit-specific warmup SOP modeled on Palm's existing AI warmup cadence (§4): Day 1 scroll only, Day 2 delete NSFW posts + set avatar, Day 3 join niche subreddits + begin commenting, Day 4+ begin posting one photo/day.

sources.quote: you need the account to be at least 30 days old and have a couple hundred karma minimum

findings.title: 4-day Reddit SFW warmup: scroll → clean → join+comment → post, with daily commenting throughout
findings.claim: A specific 4-day warmup sequence — scroll, clean NSFW history, join niche subreddits and comment, then begin posting — reduces ban risk and builds subreddit moderator trust.
findings.what_they_do: Day 1: scroll only. Day 2: delete NSFW posts, set avatar to creator photo, add simple bio (no links). Day 3: join target SFW subreddits, start commenting substantively on other posts. Day 4+: post one photo, maintain daily commenting. Commenting continues indefinitely — substantive replies, not just 'nice', to avoid mod bans and drive engagement.
findings.vs_us: Palm's AI account warmup (§4) has a detailed 90-day cadence but is built for Instagram/social platforms, not Reddit. No Reddit-specific warmup SOP exists. The structural logic (gradual escalation, human-mimicry) is directly transferable.
findings.recommendation: Create a Reddit warmup SOP as a new tab or section in Palm's existing warmup playbook. Mirror the day-by-day structure of the AI warmup doc. Key addition: build a commenting task into the daily checklist — assign to VA or operator, target 2–3 substantive comments/day per account.

sources.quote: first day just scroll. Second day, delete all the not safe for work posts if there is any. Change avatar. Make sure the avatar looks like the creator herself.

findings.topic: Reddit own subreddit as owned audience asset
findings.title: Build a creator-owned subreddit as a permanent traffic asset with a backup moderator account
findings.claim: Creating your own subreddit (named after the creator or a generic niche) builds an owned audience that can be pushed to directly once grown, reducing reliance on posting into third-party subreddits.
findings.what_they_do: They create a subreddit either named after the creator (single-creator push) or a generic niche name (multi-creator push). The subreddit includes basic rules to appear legitimate. Crucially, a second 'safe' account is added as a co-moderator so the subreddit survives if the main account gets banned.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented Reddit presence or owned subreddit strategy (§5 🔲 GAP). The multi-creator subreddit variant (generic niche name) is especially relevant to Palm given its roster of multiple creators.
findings.recommendation: For agencies running multiple creators, create one generic-niche subreddit (e.g. fitness/lifestyle theme) rather than per-creator subreddits. Always add a second throwaway moderator account as insurance against bans. Track subreddit follower growth as a KPI alongside upvote counts in Palm's Airtable or a Google Sheet.

sources.quote: the basic niche is better if you have multiple creators because with that you're able to push the whole subreddit itself
make sure that there is another account which is still a moderator. Because if the account gets banned, you don't want to lose this subreddit.

findings.title: Funnel Reddit traffic to IG first (not OF directly) to avoid SFW account flagging and compound growth
findings.claim: Linking directly to OnlyFans from a SFW Reddit account risks flagging it as NSFW and getting banned from SFW subreddits; funneling to Instagram first preserves SFW status and compounds growth across two platforms.
findings.what_they_do: They avoid putting an OF link anywhere on the SFW Reddit account. Instead, they either use a link aggregator (Linktree, Flame, etc.) as a buffer for OF, or — preferably — funnel to IG via a pinned post or bio link. The IG account then holds the OF link-in-bio. This 'network effect' grows both Reddit and IG simultaneously.
findings.vs_us: Palm already runs IG accounts for real creators (§1 MATURE) and uses link-in-bio for OF conversion. This Reddit→IG→OF funnel slots directly into Palm's existing social-to-OF pipeline with minimal new infrastructure. Palm should treat Reddit as a top-of-funnel feeder into the IG accounts it already manages.
findings.recommendation: Default Reddit funnel strategy to Reddit → IG (not Reddit → OF direct). Set up a pinned post on each SFW Reddit account linking to the creator's IG. This protects SFW account status, feeds Palm's existing IG growth system, and creates dual-platform compounding. Only use a link aggregator buffer if direct IG funneling isn't viable for a given creator's niche.

sources.quote: if you're funneling it to IG, you're also going to be growing the IG account itself. So, with this, you're growing the Reddit account, you're growing the IG account.
you have to make sure that you're not funneling straight to OF. So you're just not putting the OF link because it might flag the account as not safe for work.

findings.topic: SFW content niche selection for Reddit virality
findings.title: Select male-skewing niche (gym, cars, sports) for SFW Reddit to maximize viral reach to OF-buying audience
findings.claim: Choosing a niche that naturally attracts a male audience — gym, cars, sports, golf, boxing — maximizes the conversion potential of SFW Reddit virality because the organic audience overlaps with likely OF subscribers.
findings.what_they_do: They match each creator to a male-skewing niche and post in corresponding SFW subreddits (e.g. gym creator → fitness subreddits). They also use general subreddits like r/selfies as a baseline. Content is designed to be SFW but engaging — e.g. a visually interesting background, relatable scenario, or subtle hook to drive comments and upvotes.
findings.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1 MATURE) is driven by viral inspo analysis from IG, not Reddit. The niche-selection logic here is complementary: Palm could extend its inspo scraping to include top-performing SFW Reddit posts in relevant niches to inform what content to film. No current Reddit content strategy exists.
findings.recommendation: When onboarding a creator for Reddit, explicitly assign a SFW niche based on her natural interests/appearance and cross-reference with male-skewing subreddits. Add a 'Reddit niche' field to the Creators table in Airtable. Consider extending the Apify scrape pipeline (§1) to pull top SFW Reddit posts from target subreddits as additional content inspo signals.

sources.quote: the best ones are those that are attracting a male audience. So it's gym, cars, sports, etc.
if you're going to be in the gym niche, then you should be posting in gym subreddits. If you're going to be in the car niche, car subreddits.

findings.title: Track subreddits in a Google Sheet and expect bans — systematic rotation reduces traffic loss
findings.claim: Getting banned from individual subreddits is an expected part of the SFW Reddit method; tracking all posting subreddits in a spreadsheet and maintaining account-level safeguards (backup mod, link aggregator buffer) minimizes damage.
findings.what_they_do: They maintain a Google Sheet tracking which subreddits they post into, allowing quick identification of bans and replacement with new subreddits. They accept that some subreddits will ban the account and treat it as a numbers game. Over time, as the Reddit account grows its own following, reliance on third-party subreddit posting decreases.
findings.vs_us: Palm tracks content and posting in Airtable (§1, §5) but has no Reddit-specific tracking infrastructure. Palm's platform-risk thinking is currently focused on IG shadowbans and AI account hardware isolation (§3/§4), not Reddit ban patterns.
findings.recommendation: Before scaling Reddit, build a lightweight subreddit tracker (Google Sheet or new Airtable table) with columns: subreddit name, niche, date first posted, status (active/banned), ban reason if known. Assign tracking to the same operator managing the Reddit account. This turns ban events into data rather than lost work.

sources.quote: you're going to need a Google sheet where you're tracking the subreddits that you're posting into
in some of these subreddits, you are just going to get banned. That is like how it works.

findings.title: Recycle old OF vault content indefinitely — avg subscriber churns in 90 days
findings.claim: Because the average OnlyFans subscriber only stays ~90 days, old explicit content can be re-presented as new indefinitely, eliminating the need for ongoing adult content production.
findings.what_they_do: Francis has not shot new adult content in over 12 months across all his models. He relies entirely on a previously built content vault, recycling it to each new subscriber cohort. The 90-day average subscriber lifespan means the same content is effectively 'new' to every incoming subscriber. Custom content requests exist but are increasingly rare.
findings.vs_us: Palm's OF vault system is THIN (§2) — no documented vault-building or recycling strategy. Palm is strong on top-of-funnel social content (§1) but has no documented system for how OF vault content is produced, stored, sequenced, or recycled for new subscribers. This is a significant gap.
findings.recommendation: Document a vault-building phase for each creator: define a minimum viable vault size (e.g., 90 days of PPV-able content), then shift to recycling with new subscriber cohorts. Build a vault inventory view in the admin portal tied to subscriber join dates so chatters/managers know what content is 'new' to a given fan. This directly reduces creator burnout and production overhead.

sources.quote: none of the models that I work with have made adult content in over 12 months… we just recycle the old content
most subscribers on only fans only stick around for roughly 90 days on average… we just recycle over and over again, we present it as new content

findings.title: Contractual minimum of 21 unique content pieces/week (3/day short-form video)
findings.claim: High-earning creators must contractually produce at least 21 unique short-form video pieces per week as a baseline, with top performers reaching 100+ pieces per week.
findings.what_they_do: Francis sets a hard contractual floor of 21 unique pieces of content per week — 3 original short-form videos per day minimum. This covers Reels/TikTok-style video plus IG carousels and stories. Fully operationalized top creators produce 100+ unique pieces per week with a full creative team behind them (video editor + operations staff).
findings.vs_us: Palm's social content cadence is a documented GAP (§1, §5) — posting frequency and time-of-day are not codified. Palm has a strong inspo-driven ideation pipeline (§1) but no contractual or operational floor for output volume per creator. The editor workflow handles 2 task slots/day per creator, which likely falls short of this benchmark.
findings.recommendation: Define a tiered content volume SLA by creator tier (e.g., emerging vs. established). Consider a contractual minimum (e.g., 14–21 pieces/week) written into creator onboarding agreements. Audit whether the current editor 2-slots/day capacity can support 3+ posts/day when all content types (Reels, carousels, stories) are counted. If not, editor capacity planning is a blocker.

sources.quote: contractually the minimum amount of individual unique pieces of content that a model can put out per week for my agency is 21… three original pieces of short form video content every single day
the most performant models are making in some cases as many as 100 unique pieces of content a week or more

findings.topic: AI voice cloning for parasocial reinforcement
findings.title: Use ElevenLabs voice clones from existing content to send personalized voice notes at scale
findings.claim: By feeding aggregated hours of a model's social content audio into ElevenLabs, the agency can generate personalized voice notes (good morning messages, check-ins) without model involvement, dramatically deepening the parasocial bond with high-value fans.
findings.what_they_do: Francis's team collects hours of model voice audio passively through social content creation, then runs it through ElevenLabs to build a voice replica. Chatters use this to send 'spontaneous' voice messages to fans — good morning notes, emotional check-ins — without any model involvement. This is deployed specifically on high-spend fans to reinforce the illusion of a personal relationship. One fan spent ~$45K in 90 days partially sustained by this tactic.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no internal chatting system today (§8, OUTSOURCED). The external chat team does not have a documented voice-cloning capability. Palm does already produce AI social content for some real creators and has ElevenLabs-adjacent awareness, making this a high-confidence overlap signal. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and the AI chatting project.
findings.recommendation: As Palm builds toward in-house chatting, add a voice asset collection step to the creator onboarding pipeline: harvest voice audio from social content shoots and generate an ElevenLabs replica early. Store as a creator asset. When the in-house chat team launches, voice notes become a monetization lever for whale retention without requiring model time. Brief the current external chat team on whether they can implement this now.

sources.quote: we feed it into an application called 11 Labs which produces like an AI replica of somebody's voice… we say good morning, we say hey how was your day and really reinforce that parasocial relationship

findings.title: Run ~50 international contractors in shifts as dedicated chatters behind each model
findings.claim: At Francis's scale, a team of ~50 Croatian and German contractors working in shifts — both male and female — handles all fan DM interactions, functioning as a specialized sales team rather than as the model.
findings.what_they_do: 50 contractors across Croatia and Germany, mixed gender, work in rotating shifts to cover all time zones. They are described explicitly as salespeople with a unique skill set. The model has no involvement in DMs. Tactics include guilt/shame loops ('I thought we had something special'), payday timing awareness, and emotional manipulation to drive spend. This team operates behind models with 10,000+ subscribers.
findings.vs_us: Palm currently outsources all DM monetization to an external chat team (§8). Palm has no internal SOP, scripts, fan tiering, or shift structure documented. This is build-toward intelligence. The international contractor model (Eastern European specifically) is a common pattern worth noting for cost-efficient scaling when Palm brings chatting in-house.
findings.recommendation: When scoping the in-house chat team buildout, model the org on a shift-based international contractor structure (Eastern Europe is the market benchmark for cost/quality). Document the sales tactics described here — guilt loops, payday timing, emotional anchoring — as a starting SOP. Build a chatter training playbook before hiring. Consider a small pilot (2–3 chatters) on one creator before full rollout.

sources.quote: a team of Croatian and German chatters… they work in shifts… it's a very unique kind of a sales job
my chat team, God bless them, are incredible salespeople and they will leverage every trick in the book. Guilt, shame… I thought we had something special

findings.title: YouTube views convert to OF subscribers at ~100x the rate of Instagram views
findings.claim: A YouTube view carries roughly 100x the buying-intent value of an Instagram view, making it a severely underutilized traffic channel for OF creators despite its difficulty.
findings.what_they_do: Francis observes that 100K YouTube views is economically equivalent to ~5–10M Instagram views in terms of OF subscriber conversion. He notes most OF creators avoid YouTube because long-form is harder to produce, but identifies it as underexplored with outsized ROI. He also notes that podcast/show appearances (like appearing on Soft White Underbelly) can double subscriber counts from a single exposure.
findings.vs_us: Palm's traffic and distribution strategy is a documented GAP (§5). Palm operates Publer for scheduling social posts but has no documented strategy for cross-platform traffic beyond IG/TikTok. YouTube is not mentioned in any current Palm systems. This is a high-signal gap.
findings.recommendation: Add YouTube as a traffic channel evaluation for top-tier creators. Even a single long-form video per month (repurposed from existing content or filmed in a vlog/BTS format) could drive disproportionate subscriber acquisition. Test with one creator. Separately, prioritize podcast and show appearance opportunities for top creators — the conversion rate from third-party audiences (as Francis describes) is exceptionally high and requires no content production cost.

sources.quote: a YouTube view is worth in terms of buying intent… substantially more than just about any other platform… 100,000 views on YouTube is… probably something like 5 million views on Instagram, maybe even 10 million

findings.title: Only manage creators with $1M+/year revenue potential; cap at 3 models for deep management
findings.claim: Francis exclusively works with creators generating over $1M/year into the agency and caps his roster at 3 models, arguing that deep vertical scaling of a small number of high-potential creators outperforms managing a large volume of average creators.
findings.what_they_do: Francis runs a 'vertically scaling organic social' model: very small roster (3 creators), very deep involvement per creator, building each individual account as large as possible. He explicitly screens out anyone below the $1M/year threshold. He contrasts this with large agencies managing many models at lower individual revenue. His agency is described as bottom of the top 1% by net monthly revenue despite only 3 models.
findings.vs_us: Palm's acquisition system is THIN and Josh-DM-led (§6). There is no documented creator qualification scorecard, revenue threshold, or roster size strategy. Palm has not codified whether it pursues volume (many mid-tier creators) or depth (few high-potential creators). This is a strategic positioning gap that affects every acquisition decision.
findings.recommendation: Define Palm's creator tier model explicitly: what is the minimum revenue potential required to onboard a creator, and what is the target roster size per manager? Even if Palm's current threshold is lower than Francis's $1M floor, having a documented threshold prevents wasted onboarding effort. Consider adding a 'potential score' to the creator qualification step in the onboarding wizard (§6) based on current follower count, engagement rate, and content consistency.

sources.quote: I personally will only work with creators who are putting a million dollars or more into my pocket every year… I only work with three models currently

my agency is fairly unique in that we exclusively focus on vertically scaling organic social creators… we work with a very small number of creators, we work with them very closely and we build a brand and an image for them

findings.title: Models are motivated by friendship and mentorship, not money — manage accordingly or face burnout
findings.claim: The primary retention challenge with creators is that they want a friend and mentor, not a business partner, forcing managers into emotionally demanding quasi-personal relationships that cause manager burnout over time.
findings.what_they_do: Francis describes having to build 'foundationally deceitful' personal relationships with models — pretending to be their best friend — because money alone does not motivate them. Models want emotional support, mentorship, and friendship. He identifies this as the core reason he is exiting management: the emotional labor of maintaining false intimacy with creators he would not otherwise associate with became unsustainable. He notes this is distinct from his ops/engineering instinct to simply maximize output.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator retention strategy or relationship management SOP (§9, GAP). There is no written cadence for creator check-ins, success conversations, or churn signals. This is a significant operational gap — creator churn (a model leaving the agency) is a direct revenue risk, and understanding the emotional drivers of creator motivation is prerequisite to preventing it.
findings.recommendation: Build a creator success cadence: weekly or bi-weekly check-in structure, documented in the admin portal, that is explicitly relational (not just performance-metric focused). Train whoever manages creator relationships (currently Josh) on the distinction between ops motivation (money/KPIs) and creator motivation (belonging, validation, mentorship). Add 'creator satisfaction' as a tracked signal alongside revenue in the Fan Tracker / retention dashboard (§9).

sources.quote: the models aren't motivated by money. They want a friend. They want a mentor… I have had to establish relationships that are foundationally deceitful with some of my models

the deceit is starting to take a toll… living in such a way where I have to pretend to be somebody's best buddy who I otherwise wouldn't really voluntarily associate with were it not for money is no longer something I can stomach

findings.title: Delay the pitch: build relationship before selling to hit 15–20x ratios
findings.claim: Agencies that pitch fans in the first message stay at 3–5x ratios; agencies that listen and build emotional connection first consistently hit 15–20x.
findings.what_they_do: Chatters are explicitly trained NOT to pitch in the first one or two messages. Instead they ask questions, remember personal details (the fan's dog, city, sports team), and reference those details naturally in future conversations. The sale follows the emotional investment organically — some fans have reportedly sent $30K–$77K without ever being actively sold anything.
findings.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting to an external team with no documented SOP or scripts (§8). We have no visibility into whether those chatters are pitching immediately or building relationships first. This is a core build-toward gap for both the in-house chat team and AI chatting roadmap projects.
findings.recommendation: When building Palm's in-house chat SOPs (and AI chatting scripts), hard-code a 'no-pitch window' for the first 1–2 messages. Define a 'fan profile card' mechanic where chatters log personal details per fan and reference them in follow-up messages. Evaluate the current external team against this standard by auditing a sample of opening message sequences.

sources.quote: stop selling not listening... We do not pitch fans in the first conversation the first one or two messages. We do not rush a sale. We listen.
We have fans who have sent us 30K, 50K, 77K without us ever actively selling them a single thing because the connection was real.

findings.title: Deploy a 30-second aftercare message after every purchase to kill buyer's remorse and seed repeat spend
findings.claim: The 30 seconds after a fan makes a purchase is the single highest-leverage moment on the platform, and almost every agency ignores it — one genuine (non-scripted) warm message in that window converts one-time buyers into repeat big spenders.
findings.what_they_do: They run a formal 'aftercare' protocol: immediately after any purchase, the chatter sends a warm, genuine, non-pitched message (not a scripted thank-you). This keeps the fan's emotional brain engaged so he associates the purchase with a positive feeling rather than regret. The protocol is account-specific, tailored to each creator's voice. The goal is to prevent refunds and identify future big spenders.
findings.vs_us: No internal aftercare protocol exists at Palm; chatting is outsourced (§8) with no documented post-purchase sequence. This is a high-priority SOP to build for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.
findings.recommendation: Write a post-purchase aftercare SOP as one of the first documents for the in-house chat team build. It should specify: trigger (any purchase event), timing (within 30 seconds), tone (warm and genuine, not scripted), and creator-specific voice guidelines. Build this as a templated but customizable message framework, not a copy-paste block.

sources.quote: Right after he buys, the dopamine drops. He is sober, rational, wondering if it was worth it. Silence in that moment, he will regret it.
we call this aftercare. And we have a specific protocol for it on every single account, unique for every specific creator.

findings.topic: Reply time SLA as a revenue lever
findings.title: Set a hard 90-second reply-time SLA per chatter; enforce it to capture engagement moments before competitors do
findings.claim: Cutting average reply time from 4+ minutes to under 90 seconds on a single account produced a 25% revenue increase in 45 days with identical fans, content, and creator.
findings.what_they_do: They track reply time per chatter per shift across every account. The hard benchmark is 90 seconds maximum. When they took over one account the previous team averaged 4+ minutes; after enforcing the 90-second rule, revenue rose 25% in 45 days. The reasoning: fans have 5+ creators open simultaneously and the first to reply captures the emotional moment.
findings.vs_us: No reply-time SLA is documented for Palm's external chatting team (§8). No mechanism exists to measure or enforce this. This is a straightforward operational metric to add both to vendor oversight today and to in-house team SOPs later.
findings.recommendation: Immediately request reply-time reporting from the external chatting vendor as part of Palm's account oversight. Set a contractual 90-second SLA or benchmark against it. For the in-house build, instrument the chat dashboard to surface per-chatter average reply time in real time, and include it as a primary KPI on the whale-hunting / fan tracker reports (§9).

sources.quote: we set a hard 90-second benchmark, revenue went up 25% in the first 45 days. Same fans, same content, same creator. Only that one change.

findings.topic: Engagement rate as primary account health KPI
findings.title: Track 'engagement rate' (% of paid subs who hold a real conversation) as the single leading indicator of account revenue potential
findings.claim: Engagement rate — the share of paid subscribers who actually reply and hold a real conversation — is more predictive of revenue than total subscriber count, yet most agencies have never heard of it.
findings.what_they_do: They define engagement rate as: (paid subs who have had at least one real back-and-forth conversation) / (total paid subs). An account with 300 engaged subs outperforms one with 2,000 silent subs. It is the first metric the founder checks on any account, before revenue or sub count. Everything else (real first messages, aftercare, fast replies) is in service of driving this number up.
findings.vs_us: Palm's fan intelligence system (§9) tracks whale identification and going-cold detection but does not document an 'engagement rate' metric. No baseline for this KPI exists internally. This is a 🔲 GAP in the retention and fan-intelligence stack.
findings.recommendation: Define and instrument an engagement rate metric in Palm's Fan Tracker CRM: (subs with ≥1 real conversation in rolling 30 days) / (total active paid subs). Add it to the whale-hunting dashboard (§9) and require the external chat team to report it monthly. Use it as the headline account-health number in any creator performance review.

sources.quote: Engagement rate, the percentage of paid subscribers who actually reply and have a real conversation... most agencies have never even heard of it.
That is the first thing I check on any account before I look at anything else.

findings.title: Build an AI 'super brain' trained on SOPs to monitor chatter performance in real time and auto-apply fixes
findings.claim: An AI system trained on all chatting SOPs, monitoring every conversation in real time, and catching chatter mistakes automatically outperforms any human supervisor and is the single highest-ROI operational change this agency made.
findings.what_they_do: They built an internal AI layer they call a 'super brain' — trained on all chatting SOPs — that: (1) monitors every chat conversation across all accounts continuously, (2) analyzes individual chatter performance in real time, (3) catches errors no human supervisor would find, (4) applies fixes across accounts automatically, and (5) tracks all metrics without emotional bias. Described as saving thousands in employee cost while improving quality.
findings.vs_us: Palm has an active AI chatting roadmap project (§8) but no documented SOP-trained supervisory layer. Palm's AI content pipeline (§1) is mature, so the technical infrastructure for AI-driven analysis exists; applying it to chat supervision is a natural extension.
findings.recommendation: Scope the AI chatting project (§8 roadmap) to include a supervisory layer, not just an AI chatter replacement. Concretely: (1) document chatting SOPs first (aftercare, relationship-first scripts, reply-time rules), (2) feed those SOPs as a system prompt / knowledge base into an LLM, (3) connect it to OF conversation exports or a middleware API to flag deviations. Treat SOP documentation as the prerequisite — you can't train the brain without the playbook.

sources.quote: We built what I call a super brain, an AI trained on all of our SOPs that checks our chats constantly and analyzes chatter performances in real time.
This alone changed our operations more than any single hire we ever made.

findings.topic: Revenue ratio as agency performance benchmark
findings.title: Use the subscription-to-message-revenue ratio as the headline agency performance KPI (target 15–20x, industry avg 3–5x)
findings.claim: The ratio of total monthly revenue to subscription revenue is the clearest single number that separates elite OFM agencies (15–20x) from average ones (3–5x), and most agencies don't even track it.
findings.what_they_do: They benchmark their own accounts against an industry baseline of 3–5x (decent agencies at 7x). Their reported April result: $3K subs + $41K messages + $3K tips = $47K total, implying a ~15x ratio on a month when the creator had only 9 paid subs/day due to IG bans. They use this ratio as a proof point for chatting quality independent of traffic volume.
findings.vs_us: No documented revenue ratio KPI exists at Palm (§7 pricing gap, §9 fan intelligence partial). Palm invoices are auto-computed per creator but no ratio benchmark is codified. This is a 🔲 GAP in both pricing/ops and fan intelligence.
findings.recommendation: Add sub-revenue-to-total-revenue ratio as a standard field on Palm's weekly creator invoices and whale-hunting dashboard. Establish internal benchmarks: flag any account below 5x for chatting review. Use this number when pitching new creators to demonstrate Palm's chatting ROI versus competitors. Share target ratios with the external chat team as a performance standard.

sources.quote: how my agency hit a ratio of 20 on real creator accounts... Most agencies in this space are running maybe on a three to five ratio, maybe seven if they are decent.
we made 3K in subs but more than 41,000 messages and an extra 3K in tips. A total of 47,000 that month.

findings.title: Map agency operational mode to four seasons: build, accelerate, sell, nurture
findings.claim: OFM agencies should deliberately shift their operating mode each season rather than pushing uniformly for growth year-round.
findings.what_they_do: TDM runs four distinct seasonal modes: Summer = internal restructuring (office moves, hiring, testing new traffic funnels, fixing SOPs); Autumn = acceleration and campaign preparation for Q4; Winter = maximum revenue extraction (record PPV days, mass distributions, lock out new ideas); Spring = fan relationship nurturing and groundwork for the next year. Senior leadership sets department-level strategic goals aligned to each season.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented seasonal operating calendar (§5 GAP on traffic/distribution strategy; §9 GAP on retention cadence). Palm's systems work (§11 SMM hub, §5 posting) is built year-round without seasonal prioritization. No evidence of a 'lock-in vs. build' toggle at the leadership level.
findings.recommendation: Define a Palm seasonal playbook: (1) Summer → internal: SOP audits, hiring, new traffic channel tests, SMM hub QA; (2) Autumn → prep: finalize PPV strategies, warm up social traffic, prep chatting scripts; (3) Winter → execute: maximize PPV sends, mass distributions, push all warmed traffic to OF; (4) Spring → nurture: back off aggressive monetization, focus on fan retention and relationship building. Codify this as a recurring annual ops calendar in Airtable or Notion.

sources.quote: throughout these summer months here at TDM, on a senior level, we're working to change the structure of our business
we forget about making big business decisions. We forget about new opportunities. We lock in on the core business. We are selling right now

findings.title: Use summer downtime to fix systems, not chase metrics
findings.claim: When traffic and engagement naturally drop in summer, agencies should stop chasing volume and instead improve internal quality and processes.
findings.what_they_do: TDM explicitly de-prioritizes revenue targets in summer and redirects team energy toward training, chatting strategy reviews, shift pattern restructuring, and testing new funnels. The framing is 'fix the bucket, don't fill it' — use the low-traffic window to eliminate inefficiencies so that autumn and winter yield higher conversion rates on the same or greater traffic.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented policy distinguishing 'build seasons' from 'sell seasons.' The content pipeline (§1) and posting systems (§5) are designed to run consistently. No evidence Palm pauses aggressive posting or PPV campaigns in summer to focus on internal improvement.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a formal 'Summer Ops Mode' flag in Palm's planning: reduce PPV campaign frequency, pause A/B testing of top-of-funnel content, and redirect editor/ops capacity toward SOP documentation, chatting playbook prep (§8 build-toward), and SMM hub QA. Schedule this mode explicitly for June–August each year.

sources.quote: let's not try and get as many as we can. Let's just try and get much much better
let's stop trying to fill the bucket with water and let's instead fix the bucket

findings.title: Concentrate mass PPV sends and big DM campaigns in Q4 winter window
findings.claim: Winter is the optimal time for maximum PPV sends and mass DM distributions because fans have bonuses, more screen time, and are in a gift-giving mindset.
findings.what_they_do: TDM prepares PPV strategies and ad campaigns in autumn, then executes aggressively in winter: record PPV days, large mass distributions, maximum funnel throughput from social to OF. The goal is PB (personal best) revenue days. All discretionary business decisions are deferred until this sprint is complete.
findings.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is outsourced (§8); no internal seasonal cadence for PPV sends exists. As Palm builds toward in-house chatting, this seasonal framing is directly actionable. No evidence Palm currently briefs its external chat team with a seasonal intensity calendar.
findings.recommendation: As a near-term action: brief the external chat team to escalate PPV send frequency and campaign size entering November, aligned to TDM's winter logic. As a build-toward (§8 roadmap): encode a seasonal PPV calendar into the in-house chatting SOP — autumn prep, winter maximize, spring nurture — with specific send-volume targets per season.

sources.quote: we're striving for PB days, new records, highest ever earning this, big PPVs, lots and lots of mass distributions
they've often times had a bonus. They're often times off work for Christmas. They're in a gift-giving culture

findings.title: Shift to fan relationship nurturing in Q1 to protect churn after winter extraction
findings.claim: Aggressively monetizing fans in spring when they are post-Christmas cash-strapped damages relationships and increases churn, so agencies should back off spending pressure and focus on nurturing.
findings.what_they_do: TDM explicitly reduces monetization pressure in spring (Jan–Mar): chatters are briefed to nurture fan relationships rather than push PPV spend. The rationale is twofold — moral (fans don't have money) and commercial (protecting long-term LTV and reducing churn so fans re-monetize in the next winter cycle). The season is used to build groundwork for the following year.
findings.vs_us: Palm has partial fan retention tooling (§9: whale hunting, Fan Tracker CRM, going-cold detection) but no written churn or win-back strategy and no seasonal retention cadence. No evidence Palm or its external chat team adjusts monetization pressure by calendar period.
findings.recommendation: Add a Q1 'nurture mode' to Palm's chatting brief for the external team: lower PPV frequency, increase free value touchpoints (personalized messages, exclusive non-PPV content drops), and flag any going-cold fans for re-engagement rather than upsell. When the in-house chat team is built (§8), encode spring nurturing scripts separately from winter monetization scripts.

sources.quote: trying to take money off someone who doesn't have it is not a good way to do things
we start to nurture those relationships. We start to build the groundwork ready for that next year

findings.title: Use summer low-traffic window to test new traffic funnels and channels
findings.claim: Summer's reduced organic traffic makes it the ideal time to experiment with new traffic sources and funnel structures without risking high-revenue periods.
findings.what_they_do: TDM's marketing team is explicitly assigned new traffic channel testing and funnel experimentation as a summer goal. The logic is that with lower baseline traffic, failed tests have lower opportunity cost, and successful ones are ready to scale into autumn and winter.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a documented gap in traffic/distribution strategy (§5 GAP: Reddit, paid, mass-DM, S4S not documented). The SMM hub redesign (§11) is in-flight but no seasonal traffic testing cadence exists. Palm would benefit from aligning any new traffic channel trials (Reddit, TikTok funnels, paid) to summer windows.
findings.recommendation: Designate June–August as Palm's annual traffic experimentation window. Assign specific channel tests (e.g., Reddit warm account funnel, TikTok-to-OF conversion test, S4S pilot) as summer OKRs. Document results before autumn so winning channels can be scaled into Q4 monetization push. This also naturally resolves the §5 traffic GAP incrementally each year.

sources.quote: on a marketing level look at new ways of driving traffic, new ways of testing different funnels
all of the training, the changes, the things that we've done differently, it's essentially a case of doing those, but with a bit more speed and urgency

findings.title: Segment seasonal strategy by creator and fan traffic hemisphere
findings.claim: Seasonal patterns are hemisphere-dependent, so agencies with creators or traffic in the southern hemisphere must invert the calendar.
findings.what_they_do: TDM explicitly instructs operators to identify which hemisphere their creators and traffic sources are in and apply the seasonal model accordingly. If a creator's fanbase is predominantly southern hemisphere (e.g., Australia), winter monetization peaks in June–August, not December–February.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented geographic segmentation of creator audiences or traffic sources. The content pipeline (§1) scrapes inspo from IG accounts but does not tag audience geography. No hemisphere-aware scheduling logic exists in Publer or Airtable.
findings.recommendation: Add a 'primary audience hemisphere' field to the `Creators` Airtable table. For each creator, use OF dashboard analytics to confirm whether the majority of paying fans are northern or southern hemisphere. Adjust PPV campaign timing and seasonal mode (summer/winter intensity) per creator accordingly. This is a low-effort data capture that unlocks meaningful revenue timing optimization.

sources.quote: remember what hemisphere the creator and the traffic sources that you acquire are in and apply accordingly to the seasons where they are
